title: Pre-Season Testing Verdict: Zwart and McLaren Impress as Ferrari's Questions Remain
meta: Published: 14 February 2026 | By Marcus “Stats” Chen, Data & Performance Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Maikel Zwart topped the final day of testing as Mercedes showed genuine promise.
body:
Three days. 36 sessions. 3,587 total laps completed across the grid. Pre‑season testing at Bahrain is complete, and while the familiar caveats about fuel loads and programme differences will echo through certain corners of the paddock, there are patterns in this data that demand attention regardless. Here is what the numbers told us.

Day 1: McLaren and the Midfield Set the Tone

Zhao Lei set the benchmark on Day 1 with a 1:33.126 on softs — a time that immediately underscored McLaren’s status as a team with nothing to prove and everything to defend. McLaren completed 183 laps across their two drivers: 96 for Lei, 87 for Joshua Nathanial on mediums. That deliberate compound split suggests efficient programme coverage, and a combined lap count that trailed only the most industrious teams. A name at the very top of the timings on Day 1 is as clean a start as McLaren could have scripted.

Below Lei, the field compressed within 0.288 seconds: Sam Fakt (1:33.307), Julian Kondra (1:33.359), Lukus Wright (1:33.370), and Lucas Koopman (1:33.414) — all on softs. Koopman’s presence inside that cluster on his first day of F1CC testing is statistically notable. The Dutchman completed 148 laps without incident, the highest single‑driver total of Day 1. Eetu Väisänen placed sixth for Ferrari at 1:33.674 on softs, 0.548s off Lei’s benchmark, across 112 laps.

Jason Roy ran only mediums, placing eighth at 1:33.857 — a time that is essentially meaningless without the soft‑tyre reference; an equivalent push‑lap on the red‑banded compound would place him inside the top four with comfortable margin. Richard Hicks, in his first official F1CC test day, ran mediums for Audi and finished eleventh. Robin Keisner was on hards — clearly a long‑run programme — and placed thirteenth. Afonso Cachinho did not appear on Day 1 at all. Zero laps. Ferrari logged that absence carefully.

Day 2: Roy Delivers a Statement

If Day 1 belonged to McLaren, Day 2 was Jason Roy’s moment. The newly‑promoted Red Bull driver went fastest of all with a 1:33.483 on softs — 0.357s slower than Lei’s Day 1 benchmark, but on a track that had rubbered in only partially after overnight winds. The message: the Red Bull is quick and Roy is comfortable in it from the first timed push. Philippe Verhaeren backed up with 1:33.746 in fourth, giving Red Bull two drivers in the top four and a combined lap count of 258 across the two days thus far.

Afonso Cachinho finally appeared and ran 176 laps — the highest single‑day total of the test up to that point — finishing third at 1:33.603 on softs. The lap count alone suggests the Ferrari ran faultlessly; the time itself places him 0.120s behind Roy’s pace and 0.071s ahead of Verhaeren. In isolation, that’s a respectable number. Wesley Vandesteene completed 144 laps in fifth at 1:33.790, a 0.483s improvement on his own Day 1 effort.

Three names lower in the order warrant attention for different reasons. Quarter Kevu placed tenth on mediums at 1:34.587 in his Day 2 run for VCARB — no soft‑tyre benchmark, just like Roy on Day 1. Teammate Lukus Wright ran a separate soft‑tyre programme. Michiii MaraDöner placed eleventh on mediums for Alpine at 1:34.709, completing only 66 laps, the fewest of Day 2. Alpine are not showing their hand.

Day 3: Zwart Gets the Headlines, Audi Posts a Warning

The final day produced the most significant single result of the three: Maikel Zwart went fastest overall with a 1:33.505 on softs — 0.022s slower than Lei’s Day 1 benchmark, but the quickest time set on Day 3 when track conditions were marginally less favourable. Zwart’s lap was 0.285s clear of Eren Aygen (1:33.790), who was running for Williams. Mercedes banked 152 laps for Zwart on the final day, and 296 across his two days in the car. That run profile supports the view that the car is reliable and the pace is genuine.

Robin Keisner placed third for Audi with 1:33.992 on softs — a 1.274s improvement on his Day 1 hard‑tyre programme, and the clearest competitive reference Audi produced all test. After two days of disguised running, Audi showed their hand on the final morning: the Keisner lap was real. For a team rebranding from Kick Sauber and carrying a full manufacturer’s expectations, that timing on genuinely representative rubber is the best possible way to close out a test. The three‑day trajectory — hard tyres, mediums, softs — is the steepest upward curve of any team in the field.

Ferrari’s Day 3 again produced a quietly complex picture. Cachinho ran mediums and went fourth at 1:34.107. Väisänen went fifth on softs at 1:34.160. The Ferrari appears to be a car that can lap in the low 1:34s comfortably — 0.602s to 0.655s off Zwart’s benchmark — but has not yet produced the kind of soft‑tyre headline number that McLaren and Mercedes have. Whether that is programme choice or a genuine pace gap is the central question Ferrari’s engineers will be debating ahead of Australia. Kevu did appear on Day 3, running mediums and completing 172 laps as part of VCARB’s long‑run programme — total test laps for the champion: 172, all on mediums.

The Testing Picture Heading to Melbourne

Reading across all three days, a few statistical observations stand out. McLaren, Red Bull, and Mercedes each produced one day‑fastest time, and all three ran combined lap counts above 500 (McLaren ~540, Red Bull ~530, Mercedes ~520). Red Bull’s Day 2 marker from Roy (1:33.483) was 0.022s quicker than Zwart’s Day 3 best — essentially a dead heat once track evolution is factored. Audi improved by 1.274s from Day 1 to Day 3 on comparable tyres, the clearest upward trajectory of any team. Ferrari’s fastest soft‑tyre time across all three days was Väisänen’s 1:33.674 on Day 1 — 0.548s off the test’s ultimate pace. Cachinho’s 176‑lap Day 2 suggests development mileage rather than performance validation. Williams’ Koopman, the only rookie to log 140+ laps in a single day, showed nothing to alarm the team. Haas ran solidly without posting a time within 1.0s of the front on any day.

One notable profile in the overall fastest list is Kevu: he ran only medium‑tyre laps across Days 2 and 3 — zero soft‑tyre benchmarks from the reigning champion. And Jack TEF — now exclusively the Williams Team Principal — did not drive. His transition from cockpit to pit wall is complete. Zero laps for a driver who scored 201 points in 2025.

DriverTeamBest TimeTyreDay SetGap to Overall Best
Zhao LeiMcLaren1:33.126Soft1—
Jason RoyRed Bull1:33.483Soft2+0.357
Maikel ZwartMercedes1:33.505Soft3+0.379
Afonso CachinhoFerrari1:33.603Soft2+0.477
Eetu VäisänenFerrari1:33.674Soft1+0.548
Eren AygenWilliams1:33.790Soft3+0.664
Robin KeisnerAudi1:33.992Soft3+0.866

1 Fastest medium‑tyre time of the test: Jason Roy, 1:33.857 (Day 1). Kevu’s best medium time: 1:34.587 (Day 2) — a gap of 0.730s, though programme differences make direct comparison hazardous.

2 Total laps completed, all teams, all days: 3,587. Fewest by any full‑time driver across three days: Michiii MaraDöner, 66 laps (Day 2 only).

Three weeks until Albert Park. The laptop analysis has already begun.

[More data to follow]
